speaker: Ono
quote: If on a sunny day you climb the steep path leading up from the little wooden bridge still referred to around here as the 'Bridge of Hesitation', you will not have to walk far before the roof of my house becomes visible between the tops of two gingko trees.

speaker: Ono
quote: How so much more honourable is such a contest, in which one's moral conduct and achievement are brought as witnesses rather than the size of one's purse.

speaker: Ono
quote: I can still recall the deep satisfaction I felt when I learnt the Sugimuras, after the most thorough investigation, had deemed me the most worthy of the house they so prized.

speaker: Ono
quote: you pause at the Bridge of Hesitation and look back towards the remains of our old pleasure district

speaker: Ono
quote: you may see the line of old telegraph poles — still without wires to connect them — disappearing into the gloom

speaker: Ono
quote: today, when I try to recall that evening I find my memory of it merging with the sounds and images from all those other evenings

speaker: Ono
quote: before the war, she may still have passed for a 'young woman', but since then something inside her seems to have broken and sagged

speaker: Ono
quote: Now at last a finer, more manly spirit is emerging in Japan.

speaker: Matsuda
quote: But, Ono, there are things we should both be proud of. Never mind what people today are all saying. Before long, a few more years, and the likes of us will be able to hold our heads high about what we tried to do.

speaker: Ono
quote: Later in my life I was often to surprise colleagues with my ability to realize a scene on canvas based only on the briefest of passing glances; it is possible I have my father to thank for this skill, and the inadvertent training he gave my artist's eye during those formative years.

speaker: Ono
quote: the point I was making in the Tortoise's defence seems a very obvious one, one you may think would occur instantly to anyone with any respect for serious art.

speaker: Ono
quote: the old district had been fine. We had all enjoyed ourselves and the spirit that had pervaded the bantering and those arguments had never been less than sincere. But then perhaps that same spirit had not always been for the best. Like many things now, it is perhaps as well that that little world has passed away and will not be returning.

speaker: Ono
quote: it was Mori-san's wish at that time to do nothing less than change fundamentally the identity of painting as practised in our city.

speaker: Ono
quote: in such troubled times as these, artists must learn to value something more tangible than those pleasurable things that disappear with the morning light. It is not necessary that artists always occupy a decadent and enclosed world. My conscience, Sensei, tells me I cannot remain forever an artist of the floating world.

speaker: Ono
quote: don't you worry at times we might be a little too hasty in following the Americans? I would be the first to agree many of the old ways must now be erased for ever, but don't you think sometimes some good things are being thrown out with the bad? Indeed, sometimes Japan has come to look like a small child learning from a strange adult.

speaker: Ono
quote: I had no idea, I said, something like this would happen. I merely suggested to the committee someone come round and give Mr Kuroda a talking-to for his own good.

speaker: Ono
quote: Mr Naguchi wasn't a bad man at all, Ichiro. He was brave to admit the mistakes he'd made. He was very brave and honourable.

speaker: Ono
quote: But then I am not too proud to see that I too was a man of some influence, who used that influence towards a disastrous end.

speaker: Ono
quote: a man who aspires to rise above the mediocre, to be something more than ordinary, surely deserves admiration, even if in the end he fails and loses a fortune on account of his ambitions.

speaker: Matsuda
quote: We at least acted on what we believed and did our utmost. It's just that in the end we turned out to be ordinary men. Ordinary men with no special gifts of insight. It was simply our misfortune to have been ordinary men during such times.

speaker: Ono
quote: I would not, I resolved, revert to old habits and address him as 'Sensei'; instead, I would simply address him as though he were a colleague.

speaker: Ono
quote: It was a profound sense of happiness deriving from the conviction that one's efforts have been justified; that the hard work undertaken, the doubts overcome, have all been worthwhile; that one has achieved something of real value and distinction.

speaker: Ono
quote: But to see how our city has been rebuilt, how things have recovered so rapidly over these years, fills me with genuine gladness. Our nation, it seems, whatever mistakes it may have made in the past, has now another chance to make a better go of things. One can only wish these young people well.
